deep_analysis.executive_intro:
## EXECUTIVE INTRO

The FTSE 100 screen is broadly neutral: 71 holds dominate versus only three strong buys and four buys, with 16 avoids and six names flagged for insufficient data. Actionable value is concentrated at the top rather than spread across the index. The leading candidates cluster in cyclical, commodity-linked sectors—two Basic Materials names (Rio Tinto, Endeavour Mining) and one Energy name (Shell)—with Howden Joinery (Consumer Cyclical) and Smith & Nephew (Healthcare) rounding out the buy tier. Conviction scores are modest (32–45%) and every top pick is a new signal at one week, so persistence is unproven. A key caution: several models report missing operating cash flow, NCAV, or OCF data, and dividend yields in raw model output appear scaled incorrectly (e.g. 445% vs summary 4.5%), so qualitative follow-up and data validation are essential before sizing positions.

## TOP PICKS ANALYSIS

**Rio Tinto Group (RIO.L)** — Strong Buy on 10/22 models (composite 74%, sector-relative 77%), passing all five factor families (cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, risk). Graham Enterprising, Quality Value, Buffett Quality, High Dividend Yield, Composite Value, and Financial Health all pass on P/E 14.7, P/B 2.4, yield 4.5%, ROE 16.4%, and D/E 35%. Failures include FCF Yield (3.2% vs 5% threshold), Lynch PEG (negative earnings growth of -5.6%), Piotroski F-Score 3/9, and Earnings Quality (weak FCF conversion, missing OCF). RSI 36 and neutral timing support staged entry. With Endeavour also in Basic Materials, a combined materials sleeve would carry correlated commodity risk. **Verdict: accumulate** — broad model support and solid balance-sheet metrics, tempered by cyclical exposure and negative earnings growth.

**Endeavour Mining plc (EDV.L)** — Strongest quantitative profile among top picks: 11/22 models pass (composite 74%), including FCF Yield (17.7%), Lynch PEG (PEG 0.13, growth 105.7%), Quality Value, Buffett Quality, Economic Moat, Magic Formula, and Acquirer's Multiple (EV/EBIT 4.8). Key metrics: P/E 13.7, P/B 3.5, yield 2.9%, ROE 31.1%, D/E 20%. Graham Enterprising fails on P/B ≥ 3; Piotroski F-Score is 6/9 with missing positive OCF. Price sits below the 200-day MA with weakening MACD momentum. Sector concentration with Rio Tinto doubles Basic Materials exposure. **Verdict: accumulate** — highest model breadth and exceptional FCF yield/ROE, but gold-miner cyclicality and rich P/B warrant position limits.

**Shell plc (SHEL.L)** — Strong Buy on 9/22 models (composite 62%, sector-relative 81%), passing cheapness, dividend, GARP, and risk families but not quality. Graham Enterprising, FCF Yield (9.2%), Lynch PEG (PEG 0.48, growth 26.6%), Neff PEGY, Dreman Contrarian, and Financial Health pass on P/E 12.7, P/B 1.3, yield 3.8%, and D/E 43%. Quality Value, Buffett Quality, and Economic Moat fail on ROE 10.7% and thin margins (7.0%); Piotroski F-Score is 3/9. Conviction is the lowest of the strong buys at 34%. **Verdict: watchlist** — attractive headline valuation and cash generation, but quality-family failures and weak earnings-quality signals argue for confirmation before full allocation.

**Howden Joinery Group Plc (HWDN.L)** — Buy on 7/22 models (composite 62%), with all five factor families represented. FCF Yield (5.0%), Neff PEGY (PEGY 0.06), Quality Value, Buffett Quality, Dividend Growth, Magic Formula, and Financial Health pass on P/E 16.1, ROE 23.1%, and D/E 59%. Lynch PEG fails (PEG 1.74), Composite Value and Economic Moat fail (margins below 12%, leverage too high), and Piotroski F-Score is 5/9. P/B 3.6 limits deep-value appeal. **Verdict: watchlist** — respectable quality-income profile, but fewer model passes than strong-buy peers and consumer-cyclical sensitivity suggest waiting for clearer momentum or a better entry.

**Smith & Nephew plc (SN.L)** — Buy on 7/22 models (composite 71%) with the most favourable timing signal in the group (accumulate; timing score 0.75). Graham Enterprising, FCF Yield (9.1%), Lynch PEG (PEG 0.30, growth 70.4%), Neff PEGY, Dividend Growth, Composite Value, and Financial Health pass on P/E 21.2, P/B 2.5, yield 2.5%, and D/E 63%. Quality Value, Buffett Quality, Economic Moat, Magic Formula, and Acquirer's Multiple (EV/EBIT 14.5) fail; Piotroski F-Score is 5/9. Price is below the 200-day MA with improving MACD. **Verdict: accumulate** — timing and GARP metrics are supportive, though sub-12% ROE and failed moat screens mean sizing should stay conservative until quality concerns are resolved.

## RED FLAGS

Qualitative risks below are not captured by the quantitative screen. Where the JSON is silent, that is noted explicitly.

**Rio Tinto (RIO.L)** — High cyclicality to iron ore and industrial metals; revenue and margins swing with global demand (especially China) and capex cycles. Mining faces ongoing ESG, permitting, and community-relations risk. The screen flags negative earnings growth (-5.6%) and a weak Piotroski F-Score (3/9) with earnings-quality gaps (missing OCF), which may understate balance-sheet stress in a downturn. Governance and operational incidents (historically material for major miners) are not in the dataset.

**Endeavour Mining (EDV.L)** — Gold-miner cyclicality and jurisdiction risk: African operating exposure carries geopolitical, regulatory, and security risks not reflected in D/E 20%. Reported earnings growth of 105.7% may be non-recurring or price-driven rather than structural. P/B 3.5 and failed Graham Enterprising suggest the market already prices some quality premium. Pension, litigation, and reserve-life assumptions are not available in the payload.

**Shell (SHEL.L)** — Energy transition and climate/regulatory risk (carbon pricing, stranded-asset debate, dividend-policy scrutiny) sit outside the screen. Oil and gas earnings are inherently cyclical; ROE 10.7% and thin margins may compress in a lower-price environment. Failed quality and moat models align with a commodity business lacking durable pricing power. Legacy liabilities (decommissioning, pension obligations) are not disclosed in the JSON.

**Howden Joinery (HWDN.L)** — UK kitchen and joinery demand is tied to housing activity, renovation cycles, and interest rates—a consumer-cyclical profile the screen only partially captures via factor scores. P/B 3.6 and failed Economic Moat (leverage too high at D/E 59%) hint at limited margin of safety if volumes soften. Competitive dynamics and management succession are not in the data.

**Smith & Nephew (SN.L)** — Healthcare faces regulatory approval risk, reimbursement pressure, and product-liability exposure none of which appear in the screen. ROE 11.8% and failed moat/quality models suggest the franchise may not be as defensible as headline growth (70.4%) implies. Restructuring and market-share trends in orthopaedics/wound care require manual review; pension and litigation data are insufficient in the payload.

**Names worth deeper research:** (1) **Endeavour Mining (EDV.L)** — highest model pass rate and FCF yield, but needs jurisdiction and earnings-sustainability work; (2) **Rio Tinto (RIO.L)** — strongest dividend and composite-value profile among materials names, but commodity-cycle and earnings-quality due diligence is critical; (3) **Smith & Nephew (SN.L)** — only top candidate with an accumulate timing signal, but quality/moat failures and healthcare-specific risks warrant a focused fundamental review before adding size.

research.executive_summary:
# Endeavour Mining plc (EDV.L) — First-Pass Research Memo

**Date:** 13 July 2026 | **Sector:** Basic Materials (West African gold producer)

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Endeavour Mining screens as a **Strong Buy** on quantitative value metrics—11 of 22 models pass, with a composite score of 74% and standout FCF yield (17.7%), Lynch PEG (0.13), and ROE (31.1%) at a P/E of 13.7. The investment case rests on a step-change in 2025 financial performance: revenue doubled to $4.23bn and free cash flow surged to $1.13bn, whilst net debt fell sharply to $144m. The central debate is whether 2025 represents durable operational improvement or a gold-price-driven peak, given reported net income was negative in both 2023 and 2024 and recurring asset impairments distort headline earnings. Conviction on the screen signal is modest (42%) and timing is neutral, with the share price roughly 10% below its 200-day moving average.

---

## INVESTMENT THESIS

The quantitative screen identifies Endeavour as a rare multi-factor pass: all five metric families—cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, and risk—clear their thresholds. FCF Yield, Buffett Quality, Economic Moat, Financial Health, and Dividend Growth models all pass, suggesting the market may be under-pricing cash generation relative to balance-sheet strength and return on equity.

Business quality, on the evidence in the financials file, improved materially in 2025. Operating income rose from $715m (2024) to $1,870m (2025); gross profit margin expanded from roughly 32% to 49%; and operating cash flow reached $1,664m versus $943m prior year. Normalised EBITDA of $2,496m and normalised income of $957m indicate underlying earnings power well above the reported $679m net income, once impairments and unusual items are stripped out.

For a value investor, the hook is leverage-adjusted cash yield at a mid-teens earnings multiple: net debt of $144m against $453m cash and $1.13bn FCF implies meaningful de-risking whilst the company returned $288m in dividends and $89m in buybacks. The 2.9% dividend yield and PEG of 0.13 on screen-reported growth of 105.7% align with a GARP-plus-income profile rather than a pure commodity punt. Sector-relative composite (67%) confirms the signal is not merely a Basic Materials sector artefact.

Caveat: the Strong Buy flag is **new** (one week at signal), screen conviction is only 42%, and 2021 income-statement data in the source file is incomplete—full five-year trend analysis is limited to 2022–2025.

---

**Reported vs normalised earnings**

Reported net income was negative in three of four full years: –$57m (2022), –$209m (2023), –$300m (2024), then +$679m (2025). Normalised income tells a steadier story: $242m (2022), $166m (2023), $53m (2024), $957m (2025). EBITDA followed the same pattern: $1,199m (2022), $865m (2023), $858m (2024), $2,076m (2025); normalised EBITDA reached $2,496m in 2025. Recurring unusual items—asset impairments of $122–199m annually in 2023–2025 and special charges—materially distort headline P&L and warrant caution when applying screen P/E directly.

Debt peaked at $1.17bn in 2024 before falling 42% to $686m in 2025; net debt collapsed from $727m to $144m. Long-term debt was $555m at year-end 2025 versus $1,060m in 2024. Interest expense was $85m (2025) against operating income of $1,870m. Equity dipped in 2024 before recovering; tangible book value stood at $2.90bn (2025) with goodwill of only $134m.

Ending cash was $453m (2025), up from $397m (2024) despite net debt repayment of $527m.

**Balance-sheet strength**

Total assets were $5.61bn (2025) against total liabilities of $2.19bn; net PPE of $3.74bn dominates the asset base. Minority interests of $383m (2025) reduce attributable earnings—minority share of income was $210m in 2025.

## RISKS AND RED FLAGS

**Earnings quality.** Reported net losses in 2022–2024 despite positive operating income, driven by impairments ($122–199m per year), write-offs, and tax provisions ($210–454m). Screen ROE and P/E may reflect 2025 normalisation rather than a stable run-rate.

**Commodity cyclicality.** The 58% revenue jump in 2025 is consistent with elevated gold prices; a bullion correction would compress revenue, margins, and FCF rapidly. News flow in H1 2026 shows repeated sector selloffs tied to gold pullbacks.

**West African operating risk.** Endeavour's asset base is concentrated in Burkina Faso, Côte d'Ivoire, Mali, and Senegal (per company profile implied by news). Political instability, mining-code changes, community relations, and security incidents are not captured in quantitative screens.

**Governance and minority leakage.** Substantial minority interests ($383m equity; $210m income allocation in 2025) reduce economic interest for ordinary shareholders and complicate capital allocation analysis.

**Tax and working capital.** Cash tax payments were $362m (2025); tax payables of $496m on the balance sheet suggest ongoing fiscal complexity across jurisdictions.

**Pension.** No pension liability data present in the source file; cannot assess.

**Data gaps.** 2021 annual financials are largely absent; net debt for 2022 is missing. Screen growth rate of 105.7% may be optically inflated by the 2024 earnings trough.

**Sector context (material to EDV):**

**Notable absences:** No material M&A announcements, management changes, or regulatory enforcement actions against Endeavour specifically in the manifest. Strategy coverage is limited to capital return (buybacks/dividends) rather than production guidance or mine-life updates.

research.executive_summary:
# Shell plc (SHEL.L) — First-Pass Research Memo

**Shell plc | Energy | Screen signal: Strong Buy (9/22 models)**

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Shell plc screens as a **Strong Buy** on quantitative value metrics—P/E 12.7, P/B 1.3, dividend yield 3.8%, and debt/equity 43%—with all four factor families (cheapness, dividend, GARP, risk) passing. The investment case rests on a globally integrated supermajor trading below book with sustained capital returns (buybacks and dividends) and a balance sheet that has absorbed a full commodity down-cycle without distress. The central debate is whether normalised earnings power supports today’s valuation after revenue fell from $381bn (2022) to $267bn (2025), or whether the screen is capturing a cyclical trough that will mean-revert. Conviction on the screen signal is low (34%) and timing is neutral; deep research confirms the value case but flags commodity, transition, and pension risks that screens underweight.

---

## INVESTMENT THESIS

Shell fits a classic value-investor profile: a cash-generative, dividend-paying integrated oil major at a modest multiple with a conservative leverage profile and active share count reduction.

**Quantitative alignment.** The screen passes nine of 22 models, including Graham Enterprising, FCF Yield, Lynch PEG, Neff PEGY, Dividend Growth, Magic Formula, Acquirer’s Multiple, Dreman Contrarian, and Financial Health. Composite score is 62% (81% sector-relative), with high data quality (19/20 metrics). Key metrics—P/E 12.7, P/B 1.3, yield 3.8%, ROE 10.7%—suggest the market prices Shell as a mature cyclical rather than a distressed operator.

**Business quality supporting the screen.** Despite the post-2022 commodity normalisation, Shell has remained profitable throughout: net income of $17.8bn in 2025 versus $42.3bn at the 2022 peak. Operating margins stabilised near 10% (2024–2025) after compressing from the 2022 spike. Free cash flow of $23.9bn in 2025, though down from $45.8bn in 2022, still comfortably covers $8.5bn in dividends and $13.9bn in buybacks. Net debt of $17.1bn against $174bn in common equity implies manageable leverage (D/E 43% per screen). Share count fell from 7.0bn diluted shares (2022) to 5.9bn (2025), directly accretive to per-share metrics.

**Why Strong Buy for a value investor.** Shell combines cheapness (sub-13x earnings, ~1.3x book), income (3.8% yield plus buybacks), growth-at-a-reasonable-price characteristics (PEG-style models passing), and financial health—all four families cleared. For a patient value allocator, the stock offers exposure to a fortress-balance-sheet energy major returning surplus capital whilst trading at a discount to intrinsic asset value implied by tangible book of $148bn.

---

## FINANCIAL REVIEW

*Source: `financials_annual.json`. Note: 2021 income statement and balance sheet contain only partial line items; five-year analysis covers 2022–2025 in full.*

### Revenue and margins

Total revenue declined sequentially from **$381.3bn (2022)** → **$316.6bn (2023)** → **$284.3bn (2024)** → **$266.9bn (2025)**, a cumulative fall of ~30% reflecting lower commodity prices and normalisation after the 2022 energy shock. Operating income followed a similar arc: **$63.1bn (2022)** → **$30.7bn (2023)** → **$30.0bn (2024)** → **$27.6bn (2025)**. Operating margin compressed from ~16.5% (2022) to roughly **10.3% (2025)**, but has stabilised rather than deteriorated further in the most recent year.

### Profitability and earnings per share

Net income fell from **$42.3bn (2022)** to **$19.4bn (2023)**, then **$16.1bn (2024)**, before recovering modestly to **$17.8bn (2025)**. Diluted EPS mirrored this: **$5.71 (2022)** → **$2.85 (2023)** → **$2.53 (2024)** → **$3.00 (2025)**. The 2025 EPS uptick despite lower revenue reflects share count reduction and a lower effective tax rate (39% vs prior years). EBITDA was **$92.6bn (2022)**, **$60.4bn (2023)**, **$57.5bn (2024)**, and **$56.7bn (2025)**.

### Leverage and balance-sheet strength

Total debt was broadly stable at **$83.8bn (2022)**, **$81.5bn (2023)**, **$77.1bn (2024)**, and **$75.6bn (2025)**. Net debt moved from **$16.1bn (2022)** to **$15.5bn (2023)**, **$10.5bn (2024)**, and **$17.1bn (2025)**—still modest relative to equity. Common stockholders’ equity was **$190.5bn (2022)**, **$186.6bn (2023)**, **$178.3bn (2024)**, and **$174.4bn (2025)**; tangible book value **$164.8bn** → **$147.7bn** over the same period, partly reflecting asset base and depreciation dynamics. Total assets contracted from **$443.0bn (2022)** to **$370.4bn (2025)**, consistent with lower commodity working capital (inventory fell from $31.9bn to $22.2bn). Cash and equivalents were **$40.1bn (2022)**, **$38.3bn (2023)**, **$37.8bn (2024)**, and **$29.6bn (2025)**.

Non-current pension and post-retirement obligations stood at **$7.3bn (2022)**, **$7.5bn (2023)**, **$6.8bn (2024)**, and **$7.1bn (2025)**, with defined pension benefit assets/liabilities of **$10.2bn**, **$9.2bn**, **$10.0bn**, and **$5.1bn** respectively—material but not balance-sheet threatening.

### Cash generation and capital allocation

Operating cash flow was **$68.4bn (2022)**, **$54.2bn (2023)**, **$54.7bn (2024)**, and **$42.9bn (2025)**. Capital expenditure ran **$22.6bn–$23.0bn** annually in 2022–2024 before easing to **$18.9bn (2025)**. Free cash flow: **$45.8bn (2022)** → **$31.2bn (2023)** → **$35.1bn (2024)** → **$23.9bn (2025)**.

Shell returned substantial cash to shareholders throughout: dividends paid of **$7.4bn (2022)**, **$8.4bn (2023)**, **$8.7bn (2024)**, and **$8.5bn (2025)**; share repurchases of **$18.4bn**, **$14.6bn**, **$13.9bn**, and **$13.9bn** respectively. Net debt repayment was a financing priority in 2022–2024 before net debt ticked up in 2025 as buybacks continued alongside lower operating cash flow.

**Summary:** Shell’s five-year financial profile is one of post-peak normalisation, not distress. Revenue and FCF are well below 2022 highs, but margins, profitability, and balance-sheet metrics remain solid for a cyclical major. Capital returns have been aggressive and sustained.

---

## RISKS AND RED FLAGS

**Commodity cyclicality.** Revenue and FCF remain highly correlated with oil and gas prices. The 30% revenue decline from 2022 to 2025 illustrates earnings volatility that P/E-based screens may misread at cycle troughs or peaks.

**Energy transition and stranded-asset risk.** Long-term demand uncertainty for hydrocarbons, plus regulatory pressure on emissions, poses capital-allocation and impairment risk. The 2023 income statement records **$8.2bn** in impairment of capital assets—evidence that write-downs are not theoretical.

**Geopolitical and regulatory exposure.** Operations span jurisdictions with fiscal, environmental, and windfall-tax regimes. News flow references Middle East conflict and US–Iran developments affecting oil prices and UK-listed energy equities; these are exogenous shocks not captured in static screens.

**Pension and decommissioning liabilities.** Non-current pension obligations (~$7bn) and long-term provisions (~$21bn in 2025) represent off-income-statement commitments that reduce economic flexibility.

**Governance and strategy execution.** Shell’s pivot between “more oil” and energy-transition narratives creates investor uncertainty. Screens do not assess management quality or capital discipline on low-return renewables projects.

**FCF decline trajectory.** FCF fell 48% from 2022 to 2025 ($45.8bn → $23.9bn). If operating cash flow continues to compress whilst buybacks remain elevated, net debt could rise further from the 2025 level of $17.1bn.

**Low screen conviction.** The quantitative signal carries only **34% conviction**, is **new (one week)**, and timing is **neutral**—suggesting the Strong Buy label may not yet be battle-tested.

---

## NEWS HIGHLIGHTS

Coverage over the past year is **moderately thin on fundamental strategy** and **heavy on buyback mechanics, oil-price commentary, and retail-oriented share-price pieces**. Material themes:

**Capital returns (dominant theme).** Numerous Stock Titan reports document ongoing share buybacks and cancellations under the 2026 programme, including: *“Shell is cancelling stock it just bought back in its 2026 plan”* (27 May 2026); *“Shell buys 1.25M own shares for cancellation in one-day June buyback”* (8 Jun 2026); *“Shell plc (NYSE: SHEL) outlines June 1–11 2026 share buy-backs”* (1 Jul 2026); *“Shell continues buyback programme, cancelling more UK shares”* (11 Jun 2026). This confirms management’s stated capital-allocation priority but adds little strategic insight.

**Analyst and market sentiment.** *“Shell (UK) (SHEL) Receives a Buy from Berenberg Bank”* (10 Jul 2026, The Globe and Mail). Retail/media pieces note Shell leading FTSE rallies (*“Top Reasons Shell (LSE:SHEL) Led the UK Market Rally”*, 7 Jul 2026) and undervaluation arguments (*“1 of the UK’s most underrated stocks?”*, 22 May 2026, Yahoo Finance UK).

**Oil price and geopolitics.** Shell and BP shares moved with crude on Iran/Middle East developments: *“Shell and BP shares lifted as oil and gas price spike on Iran war”* (2 Mar 2026); *“BP and Shell shares slide as US-Iran 'framework deal' sees crude oil prices ease”* (15 Jun 2026); *“Does the Iran war spell long-term disaster for BP and Shell shares?”* (18 Apr 2026). These highlight beta to geopolitical oil shocks.

**Earnings and outlook.** *“Shell shares slide on weaker fourth quarter outlook”* (8 Jan 2026, Proactive Investors) flags near-term operational headwinds. *“Shell plc Just Beat Earnings Expectations: Here's What Analysts Think Will Happen Next”* (2 Nov 2025, simplywall.st) suggests prior-quarter beats but limited forward detail in the manifest.

**Exploration and technology.** *“Uruguay’s Offshore Oil Province Could Be Bigger Than Vaca Muerta”* (12 Jul 2026) notes Shell among majors preparing to drill offshore Uruguay. Several C3.ai articles (10–11 Jul 2026) reference an **extended enterprise AI/predictive maintenance partnership** with Shell—peripheral to the core thesis but indicative of operational technology spend.

**Coverage gap.** No manifest articles cover major M&A, CEO changes, or structural strategy shifts in depth. News is skewed toward price/buyback noise rather than fundamental research—a limitation for this first-pass memo.

---

## RESEARCH VERDICT

Verdict: accumulate  
Risk: medium  
Confidence: 0.72  
Rationale: Deep research confirms the quantitative value case—cheap multiples, strong balance sheet, and sustained capital returns—but normalised earnings and FCF are materially below 2022 peaks, commodity and transition risks are substantial, and the screen’s low conviction score warrants phased accumulation rather than full conviction on the Strong Buy label alone.
